steps.title: The Drawing Fortress
steps.text:
## Philidor — the saving technique

The **Philidor Position** (1777) is the gold-standard **defensive** method in **rook endgames**. If you remember nothing else from rook endings, remember this: when the attacker's king has **not yet crossed the 5th rank** and you can place your rook on the **6th rank** (from the defender's view), the position is a draw.

### What this endgame looks like

- The attacker has **king + rook + a single passed pawn**.
- The defender has **king + rook**.
- The defender's king sits **in front of the pawn**, on or near the queening square.

### The key concept: own the 3rd rank

The defender places his rook on the **third rank from the attacker** (his own 6th rank). This rank acts as a **wall**:

- The attacker's king cannot cross to the 6th without losing tempo to a check.
- When the attacker finally **pushes the pawn** to the 6th rank, the defender's rook drops to the **8th rank** (or 1st from the attacker's view) to **check from behind** — the king has no shelter.

### Procedure for the defender

1. Keep the **king on the queening square** of the pawn.
2. Place the **rook on the 3rd rank** from the attacker (your own 6th).
3. Wait. Make only rook moves *along that rank*.
4. The instant the pawn pushes to the 6th rank from the attacker, **swing the rook all the way back** to give endless side checks.

### Common mistakes

- Lifting the rook **off the 3rd rank** too early — the attacker's king walks in.
- Letting the **defending king leave** the queening square.
- Allowing the attacker's king to **reach the 6th rank** before you set up — at that point Lucena techniques win for the attacker.

### A model line (schematic)

... {{Ra6}} (defender's third rank) ... Kf6 ... {{Rb6+}} ... e6 {{Ra1}} (now check from behind) and the attacker's king cannot hide.

> The third rank is the wall — never leave it until the pawn does.

steps.title: The Third-Rank Defense
steps.text:
## Why the 3rd rank is everything

The **3rd-rank defense** is built on a small but inescapable logic: a king cannot advance into a square that another piece controls without first dealing with that piece.

### How the technique resolves

- While the **pawn is on its starting half**, the defender's rook holds the 3rd rank: the attacker's king has **no entry** beyond the 5th.
- The attacker has only one way to break the wall — **push the pawn** to the 6th rank himself.
- The instant the pawn arrives there, the rank is **closed off** by his own pawn — the attacker's king can no longer use it as shelter.
- The defender now drops the rook **all the way back to the 1st rank** and **checks from behind**: every check is delivered from a long distance and the attacker's king has nowhere to hide.

### What signs in your own games tell you to use it

- You are **a pawn down** in a rook ending.
- Your **king is in front of the enemy pawn**.
- The enemy **king has not yet reached the 6th rank**.
- You have a **rook with free play**.

If all four are true, set up Philidor. The half point is yours.

### Common mistakes

- Defending **passively** with the king alone — without rook coordination, the attacker's king strolls in.
- **Trading rooks** — you almost always lose the resulting K+P vs K.
- Forgetting to swing the rook back when the pawn arrives — the technique is two-stage, not one.

> Hold the third rank. Then check from behind.
